subject: Circuit installation/setup UX options when the Claude Code plugin may delegate to an external circuit-next binary
recommendation: Recommend a phased path: ship a plugin-plus-separate-CLI install with an npm-packaged CLI as the canonical distribution channel, gated behind a bootstrap check inside the plugin that detects a missing/outdated circuit-next binary and prints a single copy-pasteable install command (npm i -g circuit-next or equivalent). Rationale across the five options: (1) self-contained plugin keeps first-run success highest but blocks the architectural move to an external binary and forces every runtime change through a plugin re-release, which the brief signals is the direction of travel; (2) plugin + separate CLI install with no helper has the worst first-run UX (two-step install, version-skew confusion) and should be avoided as the default; (3) npm-packaged CLI dependency gives the cleanest release/versioning story (semver, lockfiles, global or per-project install, works on macOS/Linux/Windows where Node is already a Claude Code prerequisite, so host compatibility is effectively free) and matches how Claude Code itself is distributed; (4) bootstrap installer (curl|sh or plugin-invoked downloader) improves first-run success but introduces a second supply-chain surface, platform-specific binary builds, and an upgrade path the plugin must own forever — acceptable as a fallback but not the primary channel; (5) dev-only wrapper is fine for the inner loop but cannot be the user-facing answer. The phased path: phase 1 keep the wrapper for development; phase 2 publish circuit-next to npm and have the plugin detect-and-prompt; phase 3 evaluate a bootstrap installer only if telemetry shows the npm prompt is a real drop-off. Evidence is thin: I did not inspect the current plugin entry points, the circuit-next binary's build/packaging, or any telemetry on first-run failure rates. Confirmed facts from the provided reports are limited to the brief itself; everything about Claude Code's Node prerequisite, plugin release cadence, and host coverage is assumption-level until verified against scripts/runtime/, the plugin manifest, and the circuit-next package layout. Trustworthy proof would require: (a) confirming circuit-next can be built as a Node-resolvable package (or a prebuilt-binary npm package), (b) confirming the plugin host can shell out to a globally-installed CLI on all supported platforms, (c) a first-run script that exercises the detect-and-prompt path on a clean machine, and (d) a versioning policy doc stating how plugin and CLI semver are coupled.
success_condition_alignment: The recommendation answers the Explore goal by ranking all five named options against first-run success, maintenance, release/versioning, host compatibility, and long-term product shape; it separates the confirmed brief from assumptions about Claude Code's runtime and circuit-next's packaging; and it names the specific proof (build/packaging check, cross-platform shell-out check, clean-machine first-run script, versioning policy) that would move the recommendation from assumption-grounded to evidence-grounded.

supporting_aspects.contribution: Frames the decision as choosing a primary distribution channel for circuit-next plus a first-run UX contract, which is what lets the five options be compared on a single axis (who owns the install step) rather than as unrelated alternatives.

Names the still-missing evidence (plugin entry points, circuit-next package layout, host-platform shell-out behavior, first-run telemetry) so the recommendation is explicitly assumption-grounded and the proof path is concrete.

Separates the confirmed brief from the assumed Node-prerequisite and plugin-host capabilities, and flags the bootstrap-installer supply-chain and upgrade-ownership risk as the reason npm is preferred over curl|sh as the default channel.
